text: In 60–90 seconds, introduce yourself the way you would at a homeowner's door. What kind of work environment brings out your best, and why does a commission sales role fit you?
Tell us about a time you persuaded someone who started out skeptical. What did they object to, what did you say, and how did it end?
Sales here means hearing 'no' 40 times a day. Tell us about the longest streak of rejection or failure you've pushed through. What kept you going?
Tell us about a time a manager or coach gave you feedback you needed to act on quickly. What changed, and what was the result?
What are you looking for in your next role, and what keeps you engaged and productive long-term?
Write a text message and a short email to a homeowner who requested a free quote 48 hours ago and hasn't responded. Text under 220 characters; email under 90 words.
"Hi, I'm [Your Name] with Power — we're doing some work for your neighbors and I wanted to introduce myself while we're on the street."
A homeowner says: "I'm not interested." Read this rebuttal naturally: "Totally fair — most of our best customers said the same thing at first. Can I ask you just one question before I go?"
A bat and a ball cost $1.10 total. The bat costs $1 more than the ball. How much does the ball cost?
A lily pad patch doubles in size every day. It covers the whole pond in 48 days. How many days to cover half the pond?
If 5 machines take 5 minutes to make 5 widgets, how long do 100 machines take to make 100 widgets?
From 1 to 10, how lucky do you feel you are?
Outside of money, what do you value most in life? Give a real example of how it shows up in your daily behavior.
What does success look like 5–10 years from now, and what are you willing — and not willing — to sacrifice to get there?
In 60–90 seconds, introduce yourself the way you'd want a service customer to experience you at the drive lane.
A customer is upset their repair wasn't completed when promised. Walk us through exactly what you'd say, step by step.
How do you recommend additional services without feeling pushy? Give a real example where the customer said yes.
Walk us through how you'd explain an unexpected $1,800 repair estimate to a skeptical customer over the phone.
Tell us about feedback from a service manager you had to act on quickly. What changed and what was the result?
Write a text and a short email to a customer whose vehicle has been ready for 48 hours with no response. Text under 220 characters; email under 90 words.
"Thank you for calling McDonald Automotive service, this is [Your Name]. How can I help you today?"
"I want to get you an exact answer rather than a guess — may I place you on a brief hold while I check with the technician?"
(Voicemail) "Hi John, this is [Your Name] at McDonald Automotive with an update on your vehicle — please call me back at 303-555-0142, again that's 303-555-0142."
Parts cost $340 and labor is 2.5 hours at $180 per hour. What's the total estimate?
A farmer has 17 sheep. All but 9 run away. How many are left?
Take a number, multiply by 2, add 10, divide by 2, subtract the original number. What's the result?
From 1 to 10, how lucky do you feel you are?
Outside of money, what do you value most in life? Give a real example.
Where do you want to be in 5–10 years, and what won't you sacrifice to get there?
In 60 seconds, introduce yourself the way you'd greet a customer stepping into your shuttle.
Tell us about a time you stayed calm with a difficult or talkative passenger or customer. What did you do?
Describe your driving record honestly — tickets, accidents, anything we'd find. What should we know?
Reliability matters most here. Tell us about your attendance at your last job — and a time you showed up when it was hard to.

You have three customers to drop off: one at 9:20 near downtown Littleton, one at 9:35 by the mall, one at 9:40 close to the shop. Traffic is normal. In writing, order the route and explain your reasoning.
Your shift starts at 10:15. You need 40 minutes for prep and errands, and the drive is 15 minutes. What's the latest you can leave home?
A farmer has 17 sheep. All but 9 run away. How many are left?
What does dependability mean to you? Give a real example from the last year.
Outside of money, what do you value most in life?
In 60 seconds, introduce yourself and tell us why steady hands-on work outdoors suits you.
Tell us about a time you noticed something wrong that nobody asked you to fix — and fixed it.
Lot work is repetitive: moving cars, washing, organizing. What do you do to keep your standards high on hour six?
Tell us about a time you helped a teammate finish something that wasn't your job.

Monday morning: 8 new trade-ins are scattered across the lot, 3 sold units need to be staged for pickup, and a customer is waiting to see one car buried in the back row. In writing, what order do you work and why?
The lot starts with 24 vehicles, receives 18, and sells 29. How many are on the lot now?
A bat and a ball cost $1.10 total. The bat costs $1 more than the ball. How much is the ball?
Tell us about something you take pride in doing well even when nobody's watching.
Where do you want to be in 5 years?
In 90 seconds, introduce yourself and describe your leadership philosophy in one sentence, then the story that proves it.
Tell us about an underperforming team member you turned around. What did you do week by week, and what was the result?
Food cost jumped 4 points this month. Walk us through, specifically, the first five things you check.
Tell us about a termination you handled. What did you learn about doing it right?
Hourly turnover kills restaurants. What have you actually done that kept hourly staff longer, with numbers if you have them?
Saturday service went badly — long tickets, two walkouts, a fried cook. Write the message you'd send the team Sunday morning. Under 120 words.
Weekly sales are $42,000 and your labor target is 28%. What's your maximum labor spend?
A party of 6 splits a $138 bill evenly. How much per person?
What are you willing to sacrifice for this career — and what's off the table?
What does hospitality mean to you, in your own words?
In 90 seconds, introduce yourself and walk through your kitchen background — volumes, team sizes, cuisines.
Tell us about a food-safety issue you caught or handled. What happened and what did you change afterward?
How do you keep quality identical on portion 400 as on portion 4? Give your actual system, not a slogan.
Tell us about training a slow or struggling prep cook. What did you do, and did it work?
Tell us about feedback from an owner or chef you had to act on fast.
It's 6am. Today's prep list has 14 items, your second cook called out, and a catering order doubled overnight. In writing, how do you re-plan the morning?
A recipe yields 40 portions and the ingredients cost $118. What's the food cost per portion?
Take a number, multiply by 2, add 10, divide by 2, subtract the original. What's the result?
Why kitchens? What keeps you in this work?
Give an example of a standard you refuse to compromise on.
In 60 seconds, greet us the way you'd want a guest to experience the front door on a Friday night.
A walk-in party is furious about a 45-minute wait. What exactly do you say and do?
Servers are in the weeds and the book says seat, the floor says don't. How do you decide, and how do you communicate it?
Tell us about a time you de-escalated someone angry — guest or otherwise.
What does 'lead' host mean to you? How do you raise the game of the hosts next to you?
"Welcome to Hapa! Two tonight? We're about 25 minutes — can I grab your name and text you when it's ready?"
(Phone) "Thank you for calling Hapa Greenwood Village, this is [Your Name] — how can I help?"
A party of 6 splits a $138 bill evenly. How much per person?
Your shift starts at 4:15. You need 30 minutes to get ready and the drive is 20 minutes. Latest time to start getting ready?
From 1 to 10, how lucky do you feel you are?
Outside of money, what do you value most? Give a real example.
In 60 seconds, introduce yourself the way you'd want a first-time guest to experience you at the table.
Tell us about a difficult table you turned around. What was wrong, what did you say, how did it end — tip included, if you remember.
You have 4 tables at once: food is up for one, one was just seated, one wants the check, one needs refills. What order, and why?
How do you recommend dishes or drinks so it feels like hospitality, not upselling? Give a real example.
Doubles are long. What keeps your energy and consistency up in hour nine?
"Welcome in! Have you eaten with us before? If you're new to Malaysian food I'd love to point you to a couple of favorites."
A guest hands you $20 for a $12.50 check. How much change?
Three pizzas are cut into 8 slices each. Five people eat 4 slices each. How many slices are left?
From 1 to 10, how lucky do you feel you are?
What keeps you engaged and productive in a job long-term?
In 90 seconds, introduce yourself with the polish you'd bring to a fine-dining floor. Why does service at the highest level fit you?
Walk us through your fine-dining history: restaurants, check averages, covers per night, service style (synchronized, tableside, wine-led?).
A guest asks you to guide them through a wine pairing for two very different entrées. Talk us through how you'd do it.
Talk us through building a classic gin martini with accuracy and speed — spec, technique, glassware, garnish options.
A VIP regular's entrée comes out wrong on a full Saturday. Walk us through the recovery, step by step.
A guest has a severe shellfish allergy. In writing, describe how you'd present and safeguard one dish from your last menu for them. Under 100 words.
What's a 20% tip on a $274 check?
A party of 6 splits a $138 bill evenly. How much per person?
What does professionalism mean to you on the floor? One concrete example.
Their stated values are hospitality, integrity, professionalism, passion, leadership. Pick the one that's most you, and prove it with a story.
In 90 seconds, introduce yourself and tell us about the highest-volume operation you've helped run — sales, covers, team size.
Walk us through how you run a Friday dinner shift from pre-shift huddle to close. What do you personally watch?
Tell us about coaching a shift leader or key hourly up a level. What did you do and what happened to them?
A guest complaint hits social media overnight. What do you do the next morning?
Chains manage to numbers. Tell us about hitting a metric target without burning out the team — how?
Write the pre-shift huddle notes for a Saturday with a new menu item launching and two new servers on the floor. Under 100 words.
Weekly sales are $58,000 and labor target is 30%. Maximum labor spend?
A farmer has 17 sheep. All but 9 run away. How many are left?
What are you willing to sacrifice for this career — and what's off the table?
What kind of manager do your former teams say you were? Would we hear the same if we called them?
In 90 seconds, introduce yourself and tell us what guests would notice is different when you're the manager on duty.
You inherit a restaurant that's 6 hourly heads short going into patio season. Walk us through your 30-day staffing plan.
Tell us about the best hourly hire you ever made. How did you spot them, and where are they now?
Tell us about a hire that went wrong. What did you miss, and what changed in how you interview?
How do you keep a kitchen and a front-of-house aligned when they're blaming each other on a busy night?
Write a reply to an online applicant you want to interview this week — warm, specific, with two proposed times. Under 80 words.
A bat and a ball cost $1.10 total. The bat costs $1 more than the ball. How much is the ball?
If 5 machines take 5 minutes to make 5 widgets, how long do 100 machines take to make 100 widgets?
From 1 to 10, how lucky do you feel you are?
What does success look like 5–10 years from now?
In 90 seconds, introduce yourself and tell us about a time you ran the building when the GM was out. What broke, and what did you do?
Walk us through your experience with inventory, ordering, or scheduling — which systems, and what you improved.
Two employees no-show for a Saturday dinner shift. It's 3pm. What exactly do you do?
Tell us about a hard piece of feedback a GM gave you. What did you change?
Why assistant manager now — and what's your timeline to GM?
Write the closing-notes handoff to your GM after a rough Friday: one equipment issue, one guest comp, one staffing gap. Under 90 words.
Inventory starts at 24 cases, you receive 18, and use 29. How many cases remain?
Take a number, multiply by 2, add 10, divide by 2, subtract the original. Result?
Outside of money, what do you value most? Real example.
What won't you sacrifice on the way to running your own store?
In 90 seconds, introduce yourself with the energy you'd bring to a backyard hibachi party of 20. Show us the performer.
Walk us through your cooking background — hibachi/teppanyaki, line, catering. Where did you learn knife-and-spatula showmanship?
You arrive at an event and the venue has no working power outlet and guests arrive in 45 minutes. What do you do?
Mid-service, a guest announces a severe soy allergy. Walk us through what changes at your station immediately.
How do you read a crowd? Contrast how you'd run a kids' birthday vs. a corporate dinner.
In writing: list your load-out checklist for a 30-guest off-site hibachi event — equipment, proteins, safety items. Aim for completeness in 3 minutes.
You portion 6 oz of protein per guest for 30 guests. How many pounds do you prep? (16 oz = 1 lb)
Guests arrive at 6:00. Setup takes 50 minutes and the drive is 35 minutes. Latest departure time?
Events can't run late and can't be canceled. Tell us about your reliability — the event or shift you made happen against the odds.
Showmanship or consistency — which matters more, and why?
In 60–90 seconds, introduce yourself and tell us why you're ready to lead a shift, not just work one.
Tell us about leading people who used to be your peers — or who are older than you. How did you earn it?
Walk us through how you run a lunch rush: positioning, the line, and what you're watching for.
A teammate is slow on the make line and it's backing everything up. Coach them — right now, mid-rush. What do you say?
Tell us about opening or closing responsibility you've held: cash, keys, checklists. What did trust like that teach you?
Write the shift-handoff note to the next leader: you're low on dough, one oven is running hot, and a catering order pickup is at 5:30. Under 80 words.
A guest hands you $20 for a $12.50 order. How much change?
Three pizzas are cut into 8 slices each. Five people eat 4 slices each. How many slices are left?
Your shift starts at 10:15. Prep takes 40 minutes and your commute is 15. Latest time to leave?
From 1 to 10, how lucky do you feel you are?
Where do you want to be in 5 years, and how does leading shifts get you there?
